question.text: A company has total debt of Rs. 800 crore. After a proposed buyback, its paid-up capital will be Rs. 300 crore and free reserves will be Rs. 100 crore. Can the company proceed with this buyback?

answer.text: Yes, because the post-buyback debt-to-equity ratio is 2:1, which is within the permissible limit
No, because the debt-to-equity ratio exceeds 1:1
No, because the debt-to-equity ratio exceeds 2:1
Yes, because there is no debt-to-equity restriction on buyback

explanation.text: **The Logic:** Post-buyback debt:equity must not exceed 2:1. Here, debt = Rs. 800 crore, equity (paid-up capital + free reserves) = Rs. 300 + 100 = Rs. 400 crore. Ratio = 800/400 = 2:1, which is exactly at the permissible limit. The buyback can proceed. **The Trap:** "No, because the debt-to-equity ratio exceeds 2:1" might be selected by candidates who miscalculate or think "2:1" means the ratio must be strictly less than 2:1. The limit is "not more than" 2:1, so exactly 2:1 is permissible. **Cross-Exam Context:** Debt-equity ratio calculations appear across NISM Series IX (Merchant Banking) and NISM Series XV (Research Analyst) exams. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Model the exact post-buyback balance sheet — if the ratio is borderline at 2:1, any minor adjustment in reserves could tip it over, so build in a safety margin.
